Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, usually blocking out about three to four hours each day across the week. Being an auditory learner, I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other things or replay them multiple times. I'd set up my schedule around when I knew I could focus best, usually in the evenings after my other responsibilities. With 17 online courses, I had to be organized or I'd fall behind quickly.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, but honestly it varied quite a bit depending on what was due. When deadlines piled up, my whole routine would shift to crisis mode. I tried to stay consistent with logging in daily, but the actual depth of my studying depended on the pressure I was under that week. Having limited resources meant I couldn't always study when I wanted to—sometimes I had to wait until I could access internet at certain locations.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check announcements or see if any new lecture recordings were posted. I'm someone who learns best by listening, so I was always looking for audio content or video lectures I could engage with. Sometimes my peers would message me about something happening in the discussion forums, and that would prompt me to log in too. The peer support network we had going was actually a big motivator for me to stay connected.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became automatic to check the LMS first thing when I got online access. I'd log in, scan through my courses, check the discussion boards where my study group was active. It became routine because I knew my peers were counting on me to participate, and I didn't want to let them down. The social aspect made it feel less like a chore and more like checking in with friends.
Q5: The video lectures were gold for me—I'd watch them, sometimes multiple times, and really absorb the content through listening. The discussion forums were also incredibly valuable because I could hear different perspectives from my peers and explain concepts out loud in my posts, which helped me process information. Anything with an audio component or interactive discussion felt most aligned with how I actually learn.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped the reading-heavy PDFs and text-based resources. With my auditory learning style, staring at walls of text just didn't stick for me the same way. I'd sometimes download them intending to read later but they'd just sit there. The supplementary materials that were purely text-based felt like they were designed for a different type of learner than me.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could actually hear myself learning—like when I'd explain something in a discussion post and realize I understood it better, or when a video lecture clicked something into place. If it helped me prepare for exams or connected to what my peer group was discussing, it felt valuable. I also appreciated activities where I could see immediate relevance to the assessments.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford with my workload. Required ones got priority because they directly affected my grade, and with 17 courses, I had to be strategic. But sometimes optional discussion threads were actually more engaging because people were there by choice, not obligation. The pressure was different—optional felt like exploration while required felt like survival.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on repeat while commuting or doing chores—that's where a lot of my real learning happened. I also studied with my peer support group in person, where we'd discuss concepts out loud and quiz each other. With limited resources, I couldn't always be online, so offline study was essential for me.
Q10: Most of my deep learning happened when I was listening to downloaded lectures or talking through concepts with my study group in person. The LMS wouldn't show me pacing around my room, replaying a difficult concept until it made sense. It also wouldn't capture the hours I spent with peers, teaching each other and working through problems verbally. That's honestly where most of my understanding came from.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video lecture once, then listen to it maybe five or six times offline over the following days. My internet access isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed and then work with it offline. The LMS would show one access, but that single download might represent hours of actual study time.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, usually when multiple deadlines converged or when I was dealing with financial stress. My motivation isn't naturally high, so when external pressures mounted, it became harder to push myself. The peer support really helped during those times—knowing others were counting on me kept me showing up even when I didn't feel like it.
Q13: With 17 courses, the workload was intense and definitely affected how I used the LMS. Some days I'd log in feeling overwhelmed and just skim through things without really absorbing much. Fatigue was real—I'd sometimes watch videos but realize I hadn't actually been listening. When competing responsibilities hit, I'd prioritize the most urgent deadlines and let other things slide.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be watching a video but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other assignments or personal stuff. I'd technically be logged in and the video would be playing, but I wasn't really there mentally. Those moments usually happened when I was stressed or tired. The engagement looked real on paper but it was pretty hollow.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments more than I should have—my completion rate shows that. Sometimes the task felt too big or I didn't know where to start, so I'd avoid it. Other times I'd prioritize exam preparation over assignments because exams carried more weight. It wasn't that I didn't know they were important; I just had to make hard choices about where to put my limited energy.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my LMS usage. I'd become much more active in the days leading up to a deadline, cramming in video watches and discussion posts. My login frequency would spike around due dates. Without a deadline looming, my motivation to engage dropped significantly—I needed that external pressure to push me forward.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got positive feedback or saw my exam scores were decent, it motivated me to keep going. But when feedback was delayed or absent, I felt disconnected from my progress. The peer feedback in discussion forums was actually more immediate and kept me engaged more consistently than instructor feedback.
Q18: The course structure mattered a lot. Courses with clear weekly modules and regular video content worked well for me. When things were disorganized or heavily text-based, I struggled more. The discussion forum structure was great because it gave me a reason to engage with peers regularly. Having clear expectations helped me plan my limited study time effectively.
Q19: Video content and discussion forums made engagement easier—they matched my auditory learning style. What made it harder was when courses relied heavily on reading or when assignment instructions were unclear. Also, courses that didn't have active peer communities felt lonelier and harder to stay motivated in. The peer support intervention was actually crucial for keeping me connected.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to learn but struggling with the assignments themselves. You might watch all the videos, participate in discussions, really engage with the content—but then freeze when it comes to producing work. Maybe the assignments feel too difficult, or life circumstances make it hard to complete them. Activity doesn't always translate to output.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I did reasonably well on exams despite not completing many assignments, partly because my real learning happened offline. If someone studies effectively outside the LMS—through downloaded materials, study groups, or other resources—their low activity might not reflect their actual understanding. The LMS only sees part of the picture.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how often you show up. But they miss the quality of engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't tell if you're actually listening or just playing a video in the background. They definitely can't capture peer discussions, verbal processing, or the learning that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively processing information—listening carefully, discussing with peers, explaining concepts out loud. It's feeling connected to the course community and motivated to show up even when it's hard. True engagement is mental and social, not just clicking through content.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group meant to my success—those in-person study sessions and the accountability we built together. It wouldn't capture my auditory learning process, replaying lectures while walking around. It also wouldn't show my internal struggles with motivation or how financial stress affected my focus. The human elements of learning are invisible to the system.
